glossary.def: A unitless measure in [-1, +1] of the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables, equal to their covariance divided by the product of their standard deviations. Geometrically it is the cosine of the angle between the two mean-centred data vectors.

Pearson correlation computed on the ranks of the data rather than the raw values. It measures monotone (order-preserving) association rather than linearity, reaching +1 for any strictly increasing relationship and shrugging off outliers.

A rank-correlation coefficient defined as the difference between concordant and discordant pairs divided by the total number of pairs. It equals P(concordant) − P(discordant), is highly robust to outliers, and behaves well in small samples.

A hidden variable Z that causes both X and Y, manufacturing a correlation between them even when no direct X→Y effect exists. Adjusting (conditioning) on the confounder removes the spurious association.

A reversal in which a statistical trend present in every subgroup of the data flips direction once the subgroups are pooled. Both views are arithmetically correct; the pooled view is usually answering the wrong causal question.

A causal diagram whose nodes are variables and whose arrows are direct causal effects, with no cycles. The DAG's structure (forks, chains, colliders) dictates which variables must be adjusted for to estimate a causal effect.

Pearl's rule for choosing an adjustment set: control for a set of variables that blocks every non-causal 'backdoor' path from X to Y without opening a collider. Doing so lets observational data recover the true causal effect.

Notation P(Y | do(X=x)) for the distribution of Y when X is set by intervention rather than merely observed. Intervening severs all arrows pointing into X — exactly what a randomized controlled trial does physically.

refs.cite: Pearson, K. (1895). Note on Regression and Inheritance in the Case of Two Parents. Proceedings of the Royal Society of London 58.
Spearman, C. (1904). The Proof and Measurement of Association between Two Things. American Journal of Psychology 15(1).
Kendall, M. G. (1938). A New Measure of Rank Correlation. Biometrika 30(1-2).
Bickel, P. J., Hammel, E. A. & O'Connell, J. W. (1975). Sex Bias in Graduate Admissions: Data from Berkeley. Science 187(4175).
Simpson, E. H. (1951). The Interpretation of Interaction in Contingency Tables. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society B 13(2).
Pearl, J. (2009). Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference (2nd ed.). Cambridge University Press.
Pearl, J. (1995). Causal Diagrams for Empirical Research. Biometrika 82(4).
